data.question: Are the premiums paid for "criss-cross" life insurance tax-deductible for the business owners?

options.A: Yes, because the insurance is purchased for business purposes
options.B: Yes, if the business owners can prove the insurance relates to a business loan
options.C: No, because the insurance is not being used to secure a loan for business purposes; the death benefit is received tax-free
options.D: Yes, but only if the business is a partnership and not a corporation

data.explanation: No, because the insurance is not being used to secure a loan for business purposes; the death benefit is received tax-free.
data.kidExplanation: Because the owners get to keep the giant pile of insurance money tax-free when the other partner dies, the government says, "Nope, you don't get a tax discount on the premiums, too! You have to pay out of your own pocket."

data.question: What is a major disadvantage of using "criss-cross" insurance when the business owners have significant age or health differences?

options.A: The younger owner will refuse to participate in the arrangement
options.B: The younger/healthier owner will have to pay much higher premiums to insure the older/unhealthy owner, which can feel unfair
options.C: The older owner will not be insurable at any price
options.D: The arrangement becomes illegal under business partnership laws

data.explanation: The younger/healthier owner will have to pay much higher premiums to insure the older/unhealthy owner, which can feel unfair.
data.kidExplanation: If you are a healthy 30-year-old and your business partner is a 60-year-old smoker, it's going to cost YOU a fortune to buy a policy on their life! But it will be super cheap for them to buy a policy on you. That doesn't feel very fair, does it?

data.question: To solve the cost disparity of criss-cross insurance, a business can use "business-owned insurance." What is a key advantage of this method?

options.A: It allows each owner to eliminate their life insurance premiums completely
options.B: The costs are shared equally among the owners, and the premiums are paid with corporate after-tax dollars, which are often taxed at a lower rate than personal income
options.C: It provides coverage for the owners' personal liabilities and debts
options.D: It allows the business to deduct the full death benefit as a business expense

data.explanation: The costs are shared equally among the owners, and the premiums are paid with corporate after-tax dollars, which are often taxed at a lower rate than personal income.
data.kidExplanation: Instead of the partners fighting over who has to pay the bigger insurance bill, they just make the company buy the policies! The company pays the bills using its own money, so the cost is shared perfectly fairly between all the partners.

data.question: In private corporations, what is the role of the Capital Dividend Account (CDA)?

options.A: It is a savings account where the corporation must deposit surplus cash annually
options.B: It is a notional tracking account that allows the corporation to pass tax-free amounts (like a life insurance death benefit) to its shareholders as a tax-free capital dividend
options.C: It is a government account that holds corporate income taxes temporarily
options.D: It is a retained earnings account used only for declaring annual bonuses to employees

data.explanation: It is a notional tracking account that allows the corporation to pass tax-free amounts (like a life insurance death benefit) to its shareholders as a tax-free capital dividend.
data.kidExplanation: The CDA isn't a real bank account with actual money in it; it's a magic scorecard! It keeps track of special, tax-free money the company gets (like life insurance). When the company passes that money out to the owners, the scorecard proves to the government that the money shouldn't be taxed!

data.question: When a corporation receives a death benefit from a permanent life insurance policy (like Whole Life or UL), how much of that death benefit is credited to the Capital Dividend Account (CDA)?

options.A: The full amount of the death benefit
options.C: Only the portion of the death benefit that exceeds the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB)
options.D: None of the death benefit; death benefits do not qualify for CDA treatment

data.explanation: Only the portion of the death benefit that exceeds the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
data.kidExplanation: If your company gets a $1,000,000 life insurance payout, the government looks at the policy's "cost" (the ACB). If the ACB is $100,000, only the $900,000 difference gets to go onto the magic tax-free scorecard (the CDA).

data.question: If a cross-purchase agreement is funded by corporate-owned insurance, how do the surviving shareholders usually get the money to buy the shares from the deceased's estate?

options.A: They must obtain a loan from the bank in their personal names
options.B: The corporation receives the death benefit and pays it to the surviving shareholders as a tax-free capital dividend
options.C: The insurance company directly transfers the death benefit to the surviving shareholders' personal bank accounts
options.D: The deceased's estate lends the money to the surviving shareholders

data.explanation: The corporation receives the death benefit and pays it to the surviving shareholders as a tax-free capital dividend.
data.kidExplanation: First, the insurance company gives the big payout to the business. Then, the business uses its magic CDA scorecard to pass that exact same cash down to the surviving partners completely tax-free. Finally, the partners use that cash to buy the shares from their friend's family!

data.question: In the scenario above (funding a cross-purchase with corporate insurance), what financial instrument is often used by the surviving owners to temporarily acquire the shares from the estate before the capital dividend is paid?

options.C: A mortgage on the business premises
options.D: A lease agreement for the shares

data.kidExplanation: A promissory note is an official "I Owe You." The surviving partners hand an I.O.U. to the family to get the shares immediately. Once the business gives them the insurance cash, they use the cash to pay off the I.O.U.!

data.question: How is a "share redemption" buy-sell agreement executed when funded by corporate-owned insurance?

options.A: The corporation receives the death benefit, credits the CDA, and directly uses the funds to redeem (buy back) and cancel the shares from the deceased's estate
options.B: The surviving shareholders must purchase the shares personally with personal funds
options.C: The shares are donated to a charity and the corporation receives a tax deduction
options.D: The corporation sells the deceased's shares to a third party buyer

data.explanation: The corporation receives the death benefit, credits the CDA, and directly uses the funds to redeem (buy back) and cancel the shares from the deceased's estate.
data.kidExplanation: It's super simple! The company gets the insurance money, walks over to the deceased partner's family, and says, "Here is the cash for your shares." The company takes the shares back, deletes them, and the surviving partners are left owning the whole company!

data.question: If a business owner is uninsurable and the company cannot buy life insurance on them, how might the company fund a buy-sell agreement?

options.A: The company must immediately liquidate and distribute assets to shareholders
options.B: Through a sinking fund or by taking out a loan when the owner dies, though this is much riskier and more expensive than insurance
options.C: The company is required by law to purchase insurance even if the owner is uninsurable
options.D: A buy-sell agreement cannot legally be established without life insurance

data.explanation: Through a sinking fund or by taking out a loan when the owner dies, though this is much riskier and more expensive than insurance.
data.kidExplanation: If you can't buy an umbrella before it rains (life insurance), you have to save up your own money in a jar just in case, or run to the bank for a loan when the storm hits. It's way harder and costs a lot more, but sometimes it's the only choice!

data.question: Why is a joint first-to-die life insurance policy sometimes used to fund a business-owned buy-sell agreement?

options.A: It is required by law for all partnerships with more than two owners
options.B: It is an efficient and cost-effective option when there are exactly two shareholders, paying out upon the death of the first partner
options.C: It provides lower premiums but does not pay out if both shareholders survive to age 85
options.D: It requires the surviving shareholder to automatically sell the business to a third party

data.explanation: It is an efficient and cost-effective option when there are exactly two shareholders, paying out upon the death of the first partner.
data.kidExplanation: If there are only two partners running a business, they don't need two separate policies. They can buy one "First-To-Die" policy. It's cheaper, and it pays out the moment the first partner passes away, giving the business the exact money it needs to buy their half!
